## Technical Due Diligence Memo: Wazuh (Open Source SIEM/XDR)

### Public Evidence
- **Architecture & Platform**: Wazuh is a unified security platform offering both SIEM and XDR, with a single-agent deployment model (public evidence: [wazuh.com](https://wazuh.com/), [official architecture docs](https://documentation.wazuh.com/current/getting-started/architecture.html)). There is a clearly defined split between agent, server, indexer, and dashboard, all of which are open source ([GitHub](https://github.com/wazuh/wazuh)).
- **Product Depth & Capabilities**: Documentation and code confirm extensive endpoint, cloud, and virtualized environment support, including real-time event correlation, active remediation, MITRE-aligned detection, and policy-driven protections ([docs](https://documentation.wazuh.com/current/user-manual/capabilities/index.html)).
- **Ecosystem & Integrations**: Integrations for VirusTotal, TheHive, PagerDuty, and major cloud providers are referenced and described in public docs, with implementation via open APIs ([integration docs](https://documentation.wazuh.com/current/index.html)). Contribution guidelines and QA for user-contributed modules are published on GitHub.
- **Open Source Positioning**: Entire platform is published under FOSS license with no known license fee or vendor lock-in ([GitHub](https://github.com/wazuh/wazuh)). Strong evidence of active developer community and support channels.

### Inference
- The platform’s competitive advantage is driven primarily by transparency, cost (open source), and user/community extensibility. It likely lags proprietary SIEM/XDR platforms (e.g., Splunk, QRadar) in native AI/ML detection sophistication and advanced compliance automation, as no public evidence of advanced or proprietary analytics is visible.
- Integration design is likely standards-based, making replication by others relatively tractable; most integrations (VirusTotal, cloud APIs) leverage public schemas rather than private/proprietary methods.
- The degree of proprietary technology is highest in the unified platform, less so in third-party integrations (which carry medium wrapper risk).

### Unknowns
- The exact depth and error handling of some third-party integrations (PagerDuty, TheHive) are unconfirmed in public sources: full API surface, bidirectionality, and update/version support requires code review.
- The presence or absence of advanced, proprietary detection pipelines (e.g., ML/AI-driven anomaly or user/entity analytics) is not demonstrated in public code/docs—the product may be overshadowed here by competitors.
- No direct confirmation of privileged separation, rollback or fail-safe mechanisms in endpoint remediation logic without code review.
- Roadmap, release velocity, and rapid response to changing cloud APIs aren't visible from changelogs or blog posts (public cadence present, but not timeline commitments).

### Competitor Landscape (Public Evidence)
- **Splunk Enterprise Security**: Closed source, AI/ML detection, SOAR/UEBA features feature-gated at top tiers; not open to external code audit ([pricing/features](https://www.splunk.com/en_us/products/pricing/cyber-security.html)).
- **Elastic Security**: Open-source core, strong cloud/container support, but advanced features/compliance/reporting behind managed paywall; comparable architecture but more commercial services ([Elastic Security docs](https://www.elastic.co/security)).
- **IBM QRadar**: Fully proprietary, broad analytics and compliance, robust API/marketplace but not openly documented; high cost ([QRadar](https://www.ibm.com/products/qradar-siem)).

### Technical Substance & Wrapper Risk
- **Substance**: Wazuh offers deep, open-code SIEM/XDR with tangible, reviewable detection/protection capabilities; not a superficial wrapper.
- **Wrapper risk** is medium: core detection functionality is proprietary and native, but integrations rely heavily on public standards/APIs, making them more susceptible to replication.

### Replicability
- **Replicability is medium risk.** The open-source codebase enables rapid competitor replication (3–6 months for a capable SIEM/XDR vendor to build parity). Full plug-and-play ease may lag due to custom environment tuning required; however, advanced analytics/compliance features seen in Splunk or QRadar would not be easily copied from Wazuh.

### Evidence Gaps & Code Review Follow-Up
- **Gaps**: No detailed API reference, full integration flow, or evidence of privileged context separation in remediation logic. ML/AI analytics not proven present or absent.
- **Code Review Follow-Up**:
  1. **Integration API**: Audit implementation of third-party integrations—determine API limits and extension points; error handling, key management.
  2. **Active Response**: Confirm secure privilege separation and rollback in endpoint remediation.
  3. **Cloud Integrations**: Verify modularity, agent vs. agentless support, and update mechanisms for cloud providers.

### Summary Judgment
Wazuh is a substantial, community-led SIEM/XDR platform with fully open implementation, real multi-environment coverage, robust admin tooling, and transparent operation. Its strongest differentiators are cost and extensibility, not proprietary detection or compliance depth. Most risks are on the analytics/automation frontier, where more advanced AI/ML or closed-system compliance may be required for top-tier enterprise clients.

executive_judgment.summary: Wazuh is a mature, open-source XDR and SIEM platform with public codebase, widely demonstrated in the security community. It is a real technical asset with extensive public documentation and active open-source development. Its competitive differentiation is its open-source nature, transparent agent/platform architecture, and breadth of integrations, though it is less advanced than ‘closed’ competitors in proprietary analytics or ML. Evidence confirms Wazuh provides meaningful, maintainable security infrastructure—not just a wrapper or thin demo.
key_risks: Limited evidence of proprietary detection mechanisms or advanced ML analytics—risk that competitors with AI/ML focus (e.g., Splunk, Elastic) could surpass Wazuh in automated detection sophistication.
Strong wrapper risk for integration features: most third-party integrations are standard and not deeply proprietary (integration surface is documented, but depth is variable).
Cloud workload protection is present, but implementation could lag closed competitors in rapid support for new cloud features.
Potential that highly sophisticated/mature clients may prefer commercial platforms for advanced analytics, compliance, or out-of-the-box features.
key_strengths: Open source means unusually high transparency and auditability; clear developer and user community activity.
Broad integration support—including major clouds and security solutions—demonstrated through docs and GitHub, not just marketing.
Strong documentation and active contribution guidelines suggest maintainability and continued evolution.
Unified platform/agent architecture is well described and, with public code, is verifiable.

claims.claim: Wazuh unifies historically separate security functions into a single agent and platform architecture, offering unified XDR and SIEM protection for endpoints and cloud workloads.
claims.public_evidence: The official documentation describes a unified architecture with a Wazuh agent (deployed on endpoints), a Wazuh server for analysis, a Wazuh indexer for storage, and a Wazuh dashboard for visualization. The agent can be used on physical, virtual, containerized, and cloud workloads. The same agent provides SIEM and XDR functionality (threat detection, response, log collection, file integrity, etc.), as confirmed by the open-source docs and GitHub README.

claims.claim: Wazuh provides protection for public clouds, private clouds, and on-premises data centers.
claims.public_evidence: The GitHub documentation and official docs confirm monitoring and protection for on-premises, virtualized, containerized, and public cloud environments (AWS, Azure, GCP). There is both agent-based and API-based (agentless) support for cloud environments, and cloud security modules are included.

claims.claim: Wazuh includes both active XDR protection with real-time correlation and context, as well as on-device remediation capabilities for endpoints.
claims.public_evidence: Public documentation describes 'active response' for mitigation on endpoints, policy-based blocking, real-time event correlation, and automated countermeasures (e.g., blocking access, disabling users). These are integrated with XDR/SIEM engine. Supports both detection (MITRE, rules engine) and automated endpoint remediation.

claims.claim: Wazuh is open-source, with freely available community support, no license costs, and no vendor lock-in.
claims.public_evidence: Wazuh is published on GitHub under an open-source license (GPL/MIT for dependencies). Community contributions are encouraged, and all major components are open-source. No licensing fees are mentioned, and the core functionality is confirmed open.

claims.claim: Wazuh supports integration with third-party APIs and security solutions such as VirusTotal, TheHive, and PagerDuty.
claims.public_evidence: Official documentation and index confirm integration with VirusTotal, YARA, AWS, Azure, Docker, and generalized API/outputs for other security platforms. Not all integration details are open, but VirusTotal and other integrations are specifically referenced for malware and alert enrichment.

claims.claim: Wazuh's open-source platform allows user contributions of functional modules and code enhancements, all subjected to quality assurance checks.
claims.public_evidence: Contribution guidelines are present on GitHub, with community-driven pull requests and community-led modules/extensions. QA process is described for incoming contributions, and engagement on Slack/mailing list is promoted.

claims.claim: Wazuh's development team provides comprehensive and user-friendly documentation, along with weekly blog posts on use cases and integrations.
claims.public_evidence: Extensive documentation is available and indexed. Blog cadence itself cannot be confirmed directly, but blog and use-case documentation are present and linked from the main documentation hub.

evidence: Public documentation and architecture diagrams confirm multi-component, unified platform and agent approach.
Source code is open and available for inspection (GitHub), supporting claims of real asset status.
Capabilities such as real-time active response, policy-driven endpoint remediation, and modular third-party integrations are evidenced in docs and code.
Integrations (e.g., VirusTotal, TheHive) are supported but rely on public APIs and standards; less differentiation versus competitors in these areas.
technical_substance.reasoning: Transparent architecture, modular codebase, and clear agent-centric design are strongly backed by public, open-source evidence. Wrapper risk exists mostly for integrations, which follow industry norms, but the core detection/response functionality is native and not just a wrapper. Absence of proprietary ML/analytics is a limitation but not a structural flaw for most core use cases.

User and Entity Behavior Analytics (UEBA; Premier tier)
competitors.differentiation_notes: **Splunk is a commercially licensed product whereas Wazuh is fully open source (public evidence: Splunk pricing and packaging).** Splunk has natively integrated SOAR and UEBA in the Premier tier; Wazuh provides open-source core with integrations for security automation, but native SOAR is not clearly documented in public Wazuh docs (inference). Splunk claims use of AI-powered detection and engineering, but the actual sophistication of ML/AI methods is hard to confirm without deeper technical docs or code (inference). Splunk does not expose detailed API documentation on public pages; integration depth is presumed high based on market presence, but verification is an unknown. Pricing is per-user-based and feature-gated at higher tiers, which means some capabilities are only available at enterprise prices (public evidence: Splunk pricing table).

similar_capabilities: SIEM and XDR capabilities via Elastic Stack (Elasticsearch, Kibana, etc.)
Cloud, hybrid, and multi-cloud monitoring, posture scanning, and remediation rules
Detection rules, policy enforcement, and security analytics
competitors.differentiation_notes: **Elastic Security is similar to Wazuh in offering both open-source and managed cloud options, but Elastic's XDR and SIEM are built as extensions to their search/database platform.** Elastic exposes detailed posture management features for major clouds (AWS, GCP, Azure) and Kubernetes, and offers both agent-based and inventory scanning for vulnerabilities and compliance (public evidence: Elastic cloud security docs). Unlike Wazuh, Elastic's managed service features are mostly gated behind paid, hosted tiers, but customers can run the stack self-managed in open source. Public docs confirm cloud workload and container protection using custom agents (Elastic Defend, D4C), but the precise granularity of agent vs. agentless integration and runtime API surface is not fully documented (public evidence plus some inference). Pricing and support are feature-gated, especially in serverless and multi-cloud scale (public evidence: Elastic pricing).

similar_capabilities: SIEM/XDR analytics, including network and user behavior detection
Large public reference set of MITRE ATT&CK-aligned use cases and apps
Network, endpoint, and cloud integrations; threat intelligence feeds (IBM X-Force)
competitors.differentiation_notes: QRadar is a mature, proprietary SIEM platform, with a large ecosystem and many pre-built analytics and compliance integrations (public evidence: QRadar feature pages). It claims advanced analytics including ML for user and network behavior, as well as risk scoring, but most of these capabilities are gated behind proprietary software and require paid licensing (public evidence). QRadar's API and extension marketplace is robust but not openly documented on public pages (unknown). Wazuh offers an open-source alternative with more transparency, but QRadar’s feature breadth, especially for enterprise compliance and hybrid security, is deeper, though at a significantly higher cost (public evidence, inference).

drivers: Platform and agent codebase are openly available, lowering the technical barrier for basic replication.
Standard integration approaches and adherence to published security industry interfaces/API schemas (MITRE ATT&CK, VirusTotal, etc.).
Community support and transparent contribution model accelerate adoption and adaptation; competitors can fork or adapt.
constraints: Some integrations require custom tuning per environment, which powers users but reduces ‘plug-and-play’ ease for fast copying.
While core feature set is rapidly clonable, advanced users may require more sophisticated analytics or compliance automations, which are not openly available in Wazuh or its competitors.

sources.snippet: Wazuh provides a free, open-source security monitoring solution, unifying XDR and SIEM capabilities. Wazuh is a free, open-source security monitoring solution that unifies Extended Detection and Response (XDR) and Security Information and Event Management (SIEM) capabilities. Wazuh is an open-source security platform that helps organizations detect threats, monitor systems, and manage security events using a centralized architecture made up of four key components. The Wazuh dashboard provides a web-based graphical user interface (UI) for visualizing and managing security data, including alerts, compliance reports, threat hunting, and system health. Wazuh is often compared with leading SIEM and XDR solutions because it delivers strong threat detection and security monitoring capabilities while remaining cost-effective and highly customizable. Wazuh is an open-source security platform providing Security Information and Event Management (SIEM) and Extended Detection and Response (XDR). Wazuh is a unified platform that combines SIEM, which collects and analyzes security data, and XDR, which detects and responds to threats on endpoints and in the cloud.

sources.title: Architecture - Getting started with Wazuh
sources.snippet: The Wazuh architecture is composed of a multi-platform Wazuh agent and three central components: the Wazuh server, Wazuh indexer, and Wazuh dashboard. The agent is deployed on endpoints to collect and forward security data to the Wazuh server for analysis. * **All-in-one deployment:** All Wazuh components (server, indexer, and dashboard) are installed on a single server. * **Single-node deployment:** The Wazuh server, indexer, and dashboard are each deployed on separate servers. To start shipping this data, the agent establishes a connection with the Wazuh server service for agent connection, which listens on TCP port 1514 by default (this is configurable). Filebeat reads the Wazuh server output data and sends it to the Wazuh indexer (by default listening on port 9200/TCP). The Wazuh dashboard queries the Wazuh server API (by default listening on port 55000/TCP on the Wazuh server) to display configuration and status-related information of the Wazuh server and agents. | Wazuh server | 1514 | TCP (default) | Agent connection service |.

Architecture
The Wazuh architecture is composed of a multi-platform Wazuh agent and three central components: the Wazuh server, Wazuh indexer, and Wazuh dashboard. The agent is deployed on endpoints to collect and forward security data to the Wazuh server for analysis. The analyzed data is then forwarded to the Wazuh indexer for indexing and storage, and subsequently to the Wazuh dashboard for alerting and visualization.
Wazuh also supports agentless monitoring for systems and devices where installing the Wazuh agent is not possible. Network devices such as firewalls, switches, routers, and access points can actively forward log data via Syslog and SSH.
The Wazuh central components can be deployed in different ways, depending on scalability and availability needs:
All-in-one deployment: All Wazuh components (server, indexer, and dashboard) are installed on a single server. Best suited for labs and small environments with a limited number of monitored endpoints.
Single-node deployment: The Wazuh server, indexer, and dashboard are each deployed on separate servers. Recommended for medium environments that require higher performance than an all-in-one setup.
Multi-node deployment: Typically, one instance of the Wazuh dashboard and multiple instances of the Wazuh server (Wazuh server cluster) and indexer (Wazuh indexer cluster) are deployed on their individual servers, respectively. The number of instances varies depending on your needs. This deployment is recommended for large environments with high event throughput, or when fault tolerance and high availability are required.
Visit the installation guide and installation alternatives documentation to learn about the different ways to deploy Wazuh.
The diagram below represents a Wazuh deployment architecture. It shows how the Wazuh server and the Wazuh indexer nodes can be configured as clusters, providing load balancing and high availability.
Component communication
Wazuh agent - Wazuh server
The Wazuh agent continuously sends events to the Wazuh server for analysis and threat detection. To start shipping this data, the agent establishes a connection with the Wazuh server service for agent connection, which listens on TCP port 1514 by default (this is configurable). The Wazuh server then decodes and matches rules against the received events, utilizing the Wazuh Analysis engine.
The Wazuh messages protocol uses AES encryption by default, with 128 bits per block and 256-bit keys. Blowfish encryption is optional.
Note
Read the Benefits of using AES in the Wazuh communications document for more information.
Wazuh server - Wazuh indexer
The Wazuh server uses Filebeat to send alert and event data to the Wazuh indexer, using TLS encryption. Filebeat reads the Wazuh server output data and sends it to the Wazuh indexer (by default listening on port 9200/TCP). Once the data is indexed by the Wazuh indexer, the Wazuh dashboard is used to query and visualize the security information.
Wazuh dashboard - Wazuh dashboard/Wazuh indexer
The Wazuh dashboard queries the Wazuh server API (by default listening on port 55000/TCP on the Wazuh server) to display configuration and status-related information of the Wazuh server and agents. This communication is encrypted with TLS and authenticated with a username and password.
The Wazuh dashboard visualizes and queries the information indexed on the Wazuh indexer.

Wazuh CTI
The Wazuh Cyber Threat Intelligence (CTI) service is a publicly accessible platform that collects, analyzes, and disseminates actionable information on emerging cyber threats and vulnerabilities. This service currently focuses on vulnerability intelligence, delivering timely updates on Common Vulnerabilities and Exposures (CVEs), severity scores, exploitability insights, and mitigation strategies. It aggregates and sanitizes data from trusted sources, including operating system vendors and major vulnerability databases, to ensure high-quality, relevant intelligence.
This service is integrated directly with the Wazuh Vulnerability Detection module, but is also publicly available at the Wazuh CTI website.

evidence.note:
Wazuh is a free and open source platform used for threat prevention, detection, and response. It is capable of protecting workloads across on-premises, virtualized, containerized, and cloud-based environments.
Wazuh solution consists of an endpoint security agent, deployed to the monitored systems, and a management server, which collects and analyzes data gathered by the agents. Besides, Wazuh has been fully integrated with the Elastic Stack, providing a search engine and data visualization tool that allows users to navigate through their security alerts.
A brief presentation of some of the more common use cases of the Wazuh solution.
Intrusion detection
Wazuh agents scan the monitored systems looking for malware, rootkits and suspicious anomalies. They can detect hidden files, cloaked processes or unregistered network listeners, as well as inconsistencies in system call responses.
In addition to agent capabilities, the server component uses a signature-based approach to intrusion detection, using its regular expression engine to analyze collected log data and look for indicators of compromise.
Log data analysis
Wazuh agents read operating system and application logs, and securely forward them to a central manager for rule-based analysis and storage. When no agent is deployed, the server can also receive data via syslog from network devices or applications.
The Wazuh rules help make you aware of application or system errors, misconfigurations, attempted and/or successful malicious activities, policy violations and a variety of other security and operational issues.
File integrity monitoring
Wazuh monitors the file system, identifying changes in content, permissions, ownership, and attributes of files that you need to keep an eye on. In addition, it natively identifies users and applications used to create or modify files.
File integrity monitoring capabilities can be used in combination with threat intelligence to identify threats or compromised hosts. In addition, several regulatory compliance standards, such as PCI DSS, require it.
Vulnerability detection
Wazuh agents pull software inventory data and send this information to the server, where it is correlated with continuously updated CVE (Common Vulnerabilities and Exposure) databases, in order to identify well-known vulnerable software.
Automated vulnerability assessment helps you find the weak spots in your critical assets and take corrective action before attackers exploit them to sabotage your business or steal confidential data.
Configuration assessment
Wazuh monitors system and application configuration settings to ensure they are compliant with your security policies, standards and/or hardening guides. Agents perform periodic scans to detect applications that are known to be vulnerable, unpatched, or insecurely configured.
Additionally, configuration checks can be customized, tailoring them to properly align with your organization. Alerts include recommendations for better configuration, references and mapping with regulatory compliance.
Incident response
Wazuh agents provide out-of-the-box active responses to perform various countermeasures to address active threats, such as blocking access to a system from the threat source when certain criteria are met.
In addition, Wazuh can be used to remotely run commands or system queries on agents, identifying indicators of compromise (IOCs) and helping perform other live forensics or incident response tasks.
Regulatory compliance
Wazuh provides some of the necessary security controls to become compliant with industry standards and regulations. These features, combined with its scalability and multi-platform support help organizations meet technical compliance requirements.
Wazuh is widely used by payment processing companies and financial institutions to meet PCI DSS (Payment Card Industry Data Security Standard) requirements. Its web user interface provides reports and dashboards that can help with this and other regulations (e.g. GPG13 or GDPR).
Cloud security
Wazuh helps monitoring cloud infrastructure at an API level, using integration modules that are able to pull security data from well known cloud providers, such as Amazon AWS, Azure or Google Cloud. In addition, Wazuh provides rules to assess the configuration of your cloud environment, easily spotting weaknesses.
In addition, Wazuh light-weight and multi-platform agents are commonly used to monitor cloud environments at the instance level.
Containers security
Wazuh provides security visibility into your Docker hosts and containers, monitoring their behavior and detecting threats, vulnerabilities and anomalies. The Wazuh agent has native integration with the Docker engine allowing users to monitor images, volumes, network settings, and running containers.
Wazuh continuously collects and analyzes detailed runtime information. For example, alerting for containers running in privileged mode, vulnerable applications, a shell running in a container, changes to persistent volumes or images, and other possible threats.
The Wazuh WUI provides a powerful user interface for data visualization and analysis. This interface can also be used to manage Wazuh configuration and to monitor its status.

Wazuh server API
The Wazuh server API is an open source RESTful API that enables secure interaction with the Wazuh server infrastructure. You can interact with it using the built-in API console in the Wazuh dashboard, a command-line tool such as cURL, or any script or program capable of making web requests.
The Wazuh dashboard relies on the Wazuh server API to remotely manage the Wazuh server infrastructure. You can utilize the Wazuh server API to perform common tasks such as adding agents, restarting the manager(s) or agent(s), or retrieving detailed File Integrity Monitoring (FIM) events and status, managing users, roles, and permissions and more.
Here is a list of the Wazuh server API capabilities:
Wazuh agent management
Wazuh manager control and overview
Cluster control and overview
File integrity monitoring control and search
MITRE ATT&CK overview
Ruleset information
Testing and verification of rules and decoders
Syscollector information
Role-Based Access Control (RBAC)
API management (HTTPS, configuration)
Users management
Statistical information
Error handling
Query remote configuration
Refer to the Wazuh server API reference for details about all the Wazuh server API endpoints. For practical, real-world examples of how to use these capabilities, see the use cases section.

Cloud Security
Elastic Security for Cloud helps you improve your cloud security posture by comparing your cloud configuration to best practices, and scanning for vulnerabilities. It also helps you monitor and investigate your cloud workloads inside and outside Kubernetes.
This page describes what each solution does and provides links to more information.
Discovers and evaluates the services in your cloud environment — like storage, compute, IAM, and more — against configuration security guidelines defined by the Center for Internet Security (CIS) to help you identify and remediate risks that could undermine the confidentiality, integrity, and availability of your cloud data.
Allows you to identify configuration risks in the various components that make up your Kubernetes cluster. It does this by evaluating your Kubernetes clusters against secure configuration guidelines defined by the Center for Internet Security (CIS) and generating findings with step-by-step instructions for remediating potential security risks.
Creates an up-to-date, unified inventory of your cloud resources from AWS, GCP, and Azure. Once you connect your cloud accounts, this integration automatically finds and lists your cloud services and assets, such as:
- AWS: S3 buckets, EC2 instances, EKS clusters, and more.
- GCP: Cloud Storage buckets, Compute Engine instances, Kubernetes clusters, and more.
- Azure: Virtual Machines, Blob Storage, Azure Kubernetes Service (AKS), and more.
Read the Cloud Asset Discovery docs.
Scans your cloud workloads for known vulnerabilities. When it finds a vulnerability, it supports your risk assessment by quickly providing information such as the vulnerability’s CVSS and severity, which software versions it affects, and whether a fix is available.
Helps you monitor and protect your Linux VMs. It uses Elastic Defend to instantly detect and prevent malicious behavior and malware, and captures workload telemetry data for process, file, and network activity. You can use this data with Elastic’s out-of-the-box detection rules and machine learning models. These detections generate alerts that quickly help you identify and remediate threats.
Uses Elastic's Defend for Containers (D4C) integration to provide cloud-native runtime protections for containerized environments by identifying and optionally blocking unexpected system behavior in Kubernetes containers.

In today’s hyperconnected world, cyber criminals act with increasing agility and speed. So too must security teams. IBM QRadar SIEM helps teams meet the quick response challenge with automated, near-real-time threat detection.
QRadar SIEM can analyze millions of events in near real time by using thousands of prebuilt use cases, User Behavior Analytics, Network Behavior Analytics, application vulnerability data, and X-Force® Threat Intelligence to deliver high-fidelity alerts.
With attackers moving faster than ever, organizations must use automated threat detection to stay ahead.
IBM measured a 94% reduction in the average time for the deployment of ransomware attacks from 2019 to 2021.¹
The lifespan of phishing kits increased more than 2 times each year from 2019 to 2021.²
Containing a breach in under 200 days saves an average of USD 1.1 million.³
QRadar SIEM is purpose built to analyze both log events and network activity—this unique ability allows QRadar SIEM to provide comprehensive visibility across your security environment, including data across endpoints, on premises, cloud and network devices to limit blind spots where malicious activity could be hiding.
By extending your threat detection capabilities through an expansive set of 450 data source connectors and 370 applications for added functionality combined with network flows, QRadar SIEM monitors the full attack path often missed by other solutions with less visibility.
Log events and network activity are analyzed against historical data to uncover known and unknown threats. X-Force Threat intelligence provides outside world context to your environment to help identify threats from known malware, IPs and URLs, while User Behavior Analytics and Network Threat Analytics detect anomalous patterns by using a number of machine learning models. Thousands of use cases based on MITRE ATT&CK tactics are available for immediate use and on the X-Force App Exchange to help detect the latest attacker patterns.
When threat actors trigger multiple detection analytics, move across the network or change their behaviors, QRadar SIEM tracks each tactic and technique being used. More important, it will correlate, track and identify related activities throughout a kill chain and consolidate the data into a single alert.
Because alerts vary in gravity, QRadar SIEM uniquely determines the event priority based on a Magnitude Score, which helps security analysts focus on the most important, or most critical events, first.
The Magnitude Score is composed of 3 factors:
- Relevance: How impactful will this be to your network? (50% of magnitude score)
- Severity: What level of threat does this pose if it occurs? (30% of magnitude score)
- Credibility: With what level of integrity do you trust the data sources involved? (20% of magnitude score)
Complex algorithms are used to calculate the magnitude score. Factors such as the number of events, number of sources, age, known vulnerabilities, and risk of the data source all help to evaluate an event in your environment.
Attacks come in all shapes and sizes. Do you have the right set of use cases to detect PowerShell or lateral movement?
QRadar SIEM Use Case Manager aligns activity and rules to the MITRE ATT&CK tactics and techniques to visually highlight your depth of coverage across the attack phases.
Download use-case specific content packs for free from the IBM App exchange or build your own use cases with Use Case Manager.
User Behavior Analytics uses machine learning to determine normal user behavior against the individual and a learned peer group then flags anomalies such as compromised credentials or rogue privilege escalation and assigns the user a risk score. UBA uses 3 types of traffic to enrich and enable risk scoring:
- Traffic around access, authentication and account changes
- User behavior on the network, including proxies, firewalls, IPs and VPNs
- Endpoint and application logs, such as from Windows or Linux®, and SaaS applications
Network Threat Analytics (NTA) analyzes the flow records on your system to determine normal traffic patterns by using machine learning modeling and then compares all incoming flows to the latest baseline model. Each flow is assigned an outlier score based on the flow attribute values and how frequently the type of communication is observed. By using NTA, analysts can quickly identify which flows might indicate suspicious behavior and prioritize investigations.
QRadar Network Insights (QNI) provides a deeper analysis of the network metadata and application content within a flow. The basic level adds 18 additional attributes while the advanced level can capture details such as a malicious script or PI inside of files getting transferred through the network. By using in-depth packet inspection, Layer 7 content analysis and file analytics, QRadar Network Insights empowers QRadar SIEM to detect threat activity that would otherwise go unnoticed.
Threat detection from center to endpoint with QRadar SIEM protects your organization in a number of ways.
Incorporate IBM Security cyberthreat hunting solutions into your security strategy to counter and mitigate threats more quickly.
Integrate compliance packs into QRadar SIEM to ensure compliance and automate reporting.
Detect ransomware threats rapidly with QRadar SIEM, so you can take immediate, informed action to minimize or prevent the effects of the attack.
In 2023, 70% of cyberattacks targeted critical infrastructure industries. Check out the new report for deeper insight into attackers’ tactics.
Data breach costs have hit a new high. Get insights on how to reduce these costs.
IBM named a Leader in the 2024 Gartner Magic Quadrant for SIEM for the 14th consecutive time.
